data.title: EY investigated over audits in Post Office scandal
data.description:
EY is being investigation over its audits of the Post Office in the wake of the Horizon scandal, which saw hundreds of postmasters wrongly convicted of theft.

The Financial Reporting Council (FRC) said it would scrutinise EY’s audits of the Post Office’s accounts between March 2015 and March 2018, with specific reference to “matters relating to the Horizon IT system”.

The FRC said it had decided to open its own investigation now so as not to interfere with the wider public inquiry into the Post Office scandal, which was formally completed in December 2024.

Between 1999 and 2015, more than 900 sub-postmasters were wrongfully convicted of crimes including theft, fraud and false accounting as a result of faulty Horizon computer software that was developed by Fujitsu.

In 2024, Rishi Sunak, who was prime minister at the time, described the Post Office scandal as “one of the greatest miscarriages of justice in our nation’s history”. Fujitsu, which designed the Horizon system, was later removed from the UK Government’s list of preferred suppliers.

EY, one of the Big Four accounting firms, first started auditing Royal Mail’s accounts in 1986. The accounting giant later continued auditing the Post Office after it was split off from Royal Mail in 2012, before it was replaced by PwC in 2018.

The FRC’s probe into EY’s role in the Post Office scandal comes as the accounting watchdog’s third regulatory action against the Big Four firm within a week.

EY was fined £6.5m last week over its audits of Thomas Cook in the years before the travel company’s collapse. Just days later it was hit with a £500,000 penalty for its audit of the Scottish water company, Stirling Water Seafield Finance.

The FRC said it had “reclaimed” control of oversight over audits of the Post Office “given the heightened public interest considerations” in the wake of the Horizon scandal.

The Post Office is not officially classified as a public interest entity, meaning it is subject to less scrutiny than other large organisations that are deemed significant. It means oversight over audits of the Post Office had previously been left to the Institute of Chartered Accountants in England and Wales (ICAEW), an accounting industry trade organisation funded by its members.

An EY spokesman said: “We have been notified of the FRC’s intention to open an investigation into the EY audits of Post Office Limited for the financial years ending March 2015 to March 2018. We take our public interest responsibilities extremely seriously and will be fully cooperating with the FRC during their investigation.”

Broaden your horizons with award-winning British journalism. Try The Telegraph free for 1 month with unlimited access to our award-winning website, exclusive app, money-saving offers and more.

data.title: Television could go the way of newspapers if the US economy tips into recession, analyst warns
data.description:
As recession fears mount following Trump's sweeping new tariffs, US media and internet companies face a potential multibillion-dollar hit to ad spending that could potentially deliver the death knell to traditional television advertising.

"Given the ongoing secular headwinds facing the linear TV ecosystem, we worry that television could mirror the fate of radio and newspapers during past recessions," MoffettNathanson analysts Michael Nathanson and Robert Fishman warned in a report last week.

If a recession were to materialize, MoffettNathanson estimates US ad spending would come in $45 billion below current forecasts.

That estimate would mean an 11.5 percentage point blow to top-line revenue growth across the media landscape. Digital platforms would bear the brunt, realizing $29 billion less in ad spending, with TV facing a $12 billion shortfall.

Read more: What is a recession, and how does it impact you?

Even before the Trump administration's April 2 announcement of a 10% baseline tariff on nearly all foreign imports, MoffettNathanson said companies were already in wait-and-see mode, holding back ad spending while trade policy remained unclear.

Read more: The latest news and updates on Trump's tariffs

It's a stark reversal from the significant spending rebound the advertising industry enjoyed in 2024 on the back of record political spending, a booming economy, and lingering momentum from the post-pandemic digital and e-commerce boom.

But as MoffettNathanson argued, the tone has shifted from "softly pessimistic" to "significantly more bearish."

"The shift is driven largely by the current administration's unorthodox and aggressive approach to addressing 'unfair' trade practices," the analysts said. "The heightened uncertainty following the tariff announcement has led us to adopt a more cautious outlook for advertising."

Best and worst positioned

Companies heavily reliant on advertising — like Meta (META), Snap (SNAP), and the Trade Desk (TTD) — would be hit "disproportionately harder," with projected stock declines of at least 30% each, the firm estimates.

Digital players with variable cost structures and less reliance on advertising, like Netflix (NFLX) and Alphabet (GOOG, GOOGL), are "better positioned to weather the storm."

Meanwhile, connected TV player Roku (ROKU) would see the biggest relative impact, flipping from positive earnings in 2024 to a net loss in both 2025 and 2026, according to MoffettNathanson's projections.

Paramount could also reverse recent positive earnings trends, and other traditional media names like Disney (DIS), Fox (FOX), and Warner Bros. Discovery (WBD) would likely see earnings per share (EPS) declines of around 20% to 25%.

As earnings season kicks off, some clarity on the outlook may emerge. But if a US recession takes hold, a drop in ad spending could send lasting ripple effects across a broad spectrum of companies, regardless of their size.

As a UBS analyst said in a separate client note sent Tuesday: "Advertising is usually the first to go in an economic downturn as companies look to cut expenses."

data.title: Google faces 5 billion pound UK lawsuit for abusing dominance in online search
data.description:
(Reuters) - Alphabet's Google is being sued in the UK for potential damages worth more than 5 billion pounds ($6.64 billion) alleging the company abused its dominant market position in the online search industry.

($1 = 0.7526 pounds)

(Reporting by Akash Sriram in Bengaluru; Editing by Varun H K)

data.title: BOJ to cut growth forecast as US tariffs hit, sources say
data.description:
By Leika Kihara

TOKYO (Reuters) - The Bank of Japan is set to cut its economic growth forecasts at its April 30-May 1 policy meeting as U.S. President Donald Trump's tariffs heighten risks to a fragile, export-reliant recovery, said three sources familiar with its plans.

With markets still volatile due to Trump's back-and-forth comments on tariffs, the central bank is widely expected to hold off raising interest rates from the current 0.5%.

In a quarterly outlook report due on May 1, the board is likely to slash its economic growth forecast for the current fiscal year that began in April, the sources said on condition of anonymity as they are not authorised to speak publicly.

Under current forecasts made in January, the BOJ expects the economy to expand 1.1% in fiscal 2025.

There is no consensus within the BOJ yet on the extent of damage from Trump's tariffs, which will partly depend on whether Japan can win exemptions from U.S. tariffs in bilateral negotiations that begin this week, the sources said.

The key would be whether the BOJ can maintain its baseline scenario that Japan's economy will sustain a moderate recovery and keep underlying inflation on course to meet its 2% target, they said.

"It's clear U.S. tariffs will hurt Japan's economy. What's less clear is whether the hit is big enough to derail what has so far been a steady uptrend in inflation," one of the sources said, a view echoed by the other two sources.

So far, the dominant stance within the BOJ is that Trump's tariffs may delay, but not derail, progress in sustainably achieving its 2% inflation target - a prerequisite for further interest rate hikes.

In a newspaper interview published on Wednesday, BOJ Governor Kazuo Ueda reiterated the bank's resolve to continue raising interest rates at an "appropriate" pace.

But he also said a policy response may be required depending on the damage to Japan's economy from Trump's policies, signalling the chance of a pause in the bank's rate-hike cycle.

The BOJ may drop further hints on its views on the impact of Trump's tariffs when its board member, Junko Nakagawa, delivers a speech and holds a news conference on Thursday, analysts said.

(Reporting by Leika Kihara; Editing by Saad Sayeed)

data.title: SAIC's new EV brand co-developed with Huawei to launch first model this autumn
data.description:
BEIJING (Reuters) - Chinese automaker SAIC's new electric vehicle brand co-developed with technology conglomerate Huawei has been named Shangjie, with the first model to be launched in the autumn, Huawei's consumer business chairman, Richard Yu, said on Wednesday.

(Reporting by Beijing newsroom; Editing by David Goodman)

data.title: Big banks maintain 'the sky is not falling' amid Trump tariff turmoil. So far.
data.description:
Big banks are offering the latest view of how their customers are faring amid President Trump's tariff turmoil, and it's not all bad — at least for now.

"The message has really been that the sky is not falling," Saul Martinez, a bank analyst for HSBC, told Yahoo Finance on Tuesday.

Five of the country's largest banks over the past week posted first quarter rises in profits and revenue, driven in part by trading operations that benefited from the market volatility triggered by the initial rollout of Trump's first tariffs in February and March.

Read more: The latest news and updates on Trump's tariffs

Collectively, net profits at JPMorgan Chase (JPM), Bank of America (BAC), Citigroup (C), Morgan Stanley (MS), and Goldman Sachs (GS) amounted to $35 billion, a 13% increase from the year-ago quarter. Revenue also rose at all of these banks. Trading revenue alone jumped 17% to more than $36 billion.

Many banks said their consumer customers were still spending, defaults were still manageable, and businesses were not experiencing distress despite the uncertainty about the months ahead. None of the institutions with big Wall Street footprints updated their earnings outlooks for the rest of the year, either.

What these results didn't include, however, was the volatile period that followed Trump's April 2 "Liberation Day" tariff announcement, which took place two days after the quarter ended. Stocks and bonds plunged and have yet to fully recover, ushering in new uncertainty about the economy and the safety of US assets.

"We're surprised about the degree of ongoing firmwide strength at all 5 big banks ... and the more than expected upbeat views," said Wells Fargo banking analyst Mike Mayo in a Tuesday note.

"To us, it all says that events since Apr 2 can still be little more than a speed bump, so long as policy moves faster to help ease most of the uncertainty," he added, in reference to promised deregulation of the banking industry expected from the Trump administration.

Not that banks and their top executives were entirely upbeat. Goldman Sachs CEO David Solomon said that the prospect of a recession "has increased." JPMorgan CEO Jamie Dimon said the economy faces "considerable turbulence," with a recession as a "likely outcome."

In such a scenario, he added, "earnings won't be great and the stocks will go down."

But many bankers were noticeably more optimistic. The word "recession" did not come up once on Citigroup's Tuesday earnings calls with analysts. Jane Fraser, CEO of Citigroup, also offered an optimistic vision of the current turmoil.

"When all is said and done, and longstanding trade imbalances and other structural shifts are behind us, the U.S. will still be the world's leading economy," she said in a statement with the bank's Tuesday earnings release.

Brian Moynihan, CEO of Bank of America, went out of his way on Tuesday to note that his bank's research team doesn't see a recession happening in 2025.

Read more: What is a recession, and how does it impact you?

US consumers, he added, keep "pushing money into the economy," and the lender's business clients "remain profitable, liquid and have strong results."

But "nobody has a perfect crystal ball," Moynihan acknowledged, noting that "we potentially face a changing economy in the future."

Some banks even showed a rise in investment banking income during the first quarter, defying predictions of a slowdown. Executives acknowledged some deals were now on hold due to uncertainties, but many described the pullbacks as temporary rather than long-lasting.

"I'm saying pause versus delete," Morgan Stanley CEO Ted Pick told analysts of the current investment banking environment. "We don't know whether the economy is going to contract."

Even amid the optimism, there were signs that banks are preparing for harder times. JPMorgan, Bank of America, Citigroup, and Wells Fargo (WFC) added $8.4 billion in provisions to account for future loan losses.

The figure was 29% higher than the year-ago period and 9% higher than the previous quarter.

"We're still in a cloudy period. It's going to be choppy from here, and it's exhausting,” Ken Leon, an analyst for CFRA Research, told Yahoo Finance.

David Hollerith is a senior reporter for Yahoo Finance covering banking, crypto, and other areas in finance.

Click here for in-depth analysis of the latest stock market news and events moving stock prices

Read the latest financial and business news from Yahoo Finance

data.title: Ferrari will continue to invest in petrol, hybrid and EV powetrains, CEO says
data.description:
MILAN (Reuters) - Luxury sportscar maker Ferrari will continue to develop petrol, hybrid and full-electric vehicles, its Chief Executive Bendetto Vigna said on Wednesday during the company's annual shareholders' meeting.

"Indeed ... we continue to invest in all three powertrains - internal combustion engine, hybrid, and full electric - to offer our clients maximum freedom of choice," Vigna said.

Known globally for its roaring petrol engines, Ferrari started selling hybrid models in 2019 and will unveil its fist fully-electric (EV) model in October.

(Reporting by Giulio Piovaccari, editing by Cristina Carlevaro)

data.title: Mortgage increase calculator: find out how much your bills could rise
data.description:
This tool shows how much a new mortgage deal will impact your monthly payments.

Mortgage increase calculator

How to use this calculator

Enter the amount you have left to pay on your mortgage, the number of years you have left in which to pay it, your annual pre-tax income (you can enter your household income if more than one person contributes to the mortgage) and the interest rate on your current mortgage deal.

Then, using the slider, select the interest rate for your new mortgage deal.

The results will show you your current and future mortgage payments, the difference between and two, and the proportion of your take-home pay that will be taken up by these mortgage payments.

How it works

The calculator uses the information you provide about your remaining loan, mortgage term and interest rate to work out your monthly payments.

The difference between your old and new mortgage deal is picked out so you can see how much your bills will increase, and each figure is set against your monthly income to show how much is being taken up by mortgage bills alone.

What is this calculator useful for?

This is useful for anyone who needs to remortgage and wants to know how their monthly bills will change. If your bills are set to change dramatically, you can at least start planning ahead of time.

If you’re trying to choose between more than one deal, you can easily change the information to see how your monthly payments would differ, and whether you could free up more of your take home pay.

What’s happening to mortgage rates?

The Bank of England reduced interest rates to 4.5pc at the start of February, but held rates at its most recent meeting in March.

Further rate reductions are expected this year – with markets pricing in three, and a possible fourth – but they’ll have to be offset against the UK’s issue with stubborn inflation. The Consumer Prices Index (CPI) measure of inflation eased to 2.6pc in March, down slightly from 2.8pc in February – but still well ahead of the Bank’s 2pc target.

The next Bank Rate decision is on May 8.

data.title: Xiaomi will not bring new YU7 to China's largest auto show, exec says
data.description:
SHANGHAI (Reuters) - Xiaomi does not plan to showcase its upcoming YU7 electric SUV at China's largest auto show next week, deflating widespread anticipation among visitors keen to catch a glimpse of the would-be challenger to Tesla's Model Y.

Xiaomi's Vice President Li Xiaoshuang said the company would only take its SU7 and SU7 Ultra vehicles to the Shanghai auto show on social media platform Weibo on Tuesday.

Asked by users about the YU7 at the show, he said it would not be featured. "Don't be anxious, when it arrives it will be in its best state," Li wrote in one of the responses.

Several industry bloggers have in the weeks leading up to the show published articles saying that the YU7 would be a highlight of the event, if it was shown.

Xiaomi founder and CEO Lei Jun has previously said the plan was to officially launch the YU7 in June or July but has been regularly posting pictures of the SUV on his widely followed Weibo account, which has more than 26.5 million followers.

Analysts have said they expect the YU7 to be the strongest competitor in the Chinese market for Tesla's best-selling Model Y.

Xiaomi began manufacturing EVs last year with the launch of the SU7 sedan after selling smartphones, household appliances and smart gadgets for most of its 15-year history. Since December, the SU7 sedan has outsold Tesla's Model 3 on a monthly basis.

The company was thrust into controversy last month after a fatal accident involving an SU7, which caught fire after hitting a roadside pole at a speed of 97 km/hr while its advance driving assistance system was switched on, killing the driver and two other passengers.

The accident triggered wide discussions about EV safety in China and the country's state media has since urged automakers not to overplay smart-driving capabilities.

(Reporting by Zhang Yan, Brenda Goh; Editing by Saad Sayeed)

data.title: Everything you need to know about remortgaging your home
data.description:
If your mortgage deal is coming to an end in the next few months, it is time to start the process of remortgaging to switch to a new one.

Getting started early means you’ll avoid ending up on your lender’s standard variable rate, SVR, which is usually much more expensive.

Here, Telegraph Money explains how the remortgaging process works, and how to get the best rate for your mortgage.

  • What is remortgaging?

  • Remortgaging process

  • Where can I find the cheapest interest rates?

  • How long should I lock in a rate for?

  • How can I get the best rate for my mortgage?

What is remortgaging?

Remortgaging refers to taking out a new mortgage deal whenever the fixed rate expires – so, for example, finding a new two-year fix when your current two-year fix runs out.

Technically, you only remortgage if you’re moving to a different lender, but the term can also be used to describe those switching to a different deal with the same lender – but this is actually a “product transfer”.

Why should you remortgage?

There are lots of reasons why you should remortgage, including:

  • Finding a competitive deal – The cheapest rates tend to be on offer from fixed-rate or tracker mortgages, so it’s a good idea to switch straight from one deal to another. Not only will your monthly repayments be lower, you’ll also pay less interest over the course of the mortgage term. You may also save money if your home’s value has increased; the extra equity will mean you could be borrowing a smaller proportion of the total property cost – and a 60pc loan tends to be cheaper than a 75pc loan, for example.

  • Avoiding your lender’s standard variable rate (SVR) – A lender’s SVR is the most expensive option, and can also fluctuate at the lender’s discretion. In general, these rates will go up and down in accordance with the Bank Rate, but they’re not officially linked.

  • Protecting yourself against fluctuations – Opting for a fixed-rate deal means you’ll have the peace of mind of knowing what your repayments will be for the duration of the term. This can be particularly beneficial if mortgage rates rise during the term.

  • Borrowing more – If you want to remove equity from your home, you’ll need to go through the remortgaging process so a lender can decide whether or not to lend you more.

However, there are some instances where it could be worth allowing your mortgage to revert to your lender’s SVR – for example, if you’re planning to move house in the near future and cannot port your mortgage to another property, and if you’re due to finish repaying your mortgage soon.

In these sorts of cases, the fees you’ll pay for taking out a new deal and possible early repayment charges may not be worth it.

Remortgaging process

1. Start looking around – six months in advance

The remortgaging process can take a while, so if you want to avoid spending time on your lender’s SVR, it’s a good idea to get the process started a few months before your current deal is due to end.

It’s a good idea to make a note of when this will be, but your lender should also get in touch with you well in advance of your deal ending.

You might want to contact a mortgage broker to start looking for deals in advance – some lenders will let you hold a mortgage in principle for up to six months. Though some have cut this back to three or four months.

2. Ask for a closing balance

Also known as a redemption statement, your lender will issue a statement that shows how much is on your remaining mortgage loan, which you’ll need to borrow if you remortgage.

This information will be required when you make an application to a new lender.

3. Decide on a new mortgage

A mortgage broker can help you make this decision, and you might find our guide on whether to choose a fixed-rate or tracker mortgage helpful.

Alternatively, you might want to apply for an interest-only mortgage.

Your broker, if you’re using one, can start the application process once you’ve spotted a deal you want.

4. Instruct a solicitor

This is a necessary step if you’re moving to a different lender. A solicitor or conveyancer will sort the paperwork required for the new contract, such as drawing up the mortgage deeds.

5. Go through affordability checks

Your new lender will need to check that you can afford the mortgage you’re applying for. If you’re simply moving from one repayment mortgage to another, the process should be relatively straightforward, as you’ll likely need to borrow less than you did at the start of your previous deal.

However, if you’re moving to an interest-only deal, or want to remortgage to take equity out of your property, then the affordability checks may be more rigorous.

You’ll likely need some of the following documents:

  • Recent payslips

  • Utility bills

  • Bank statements

  • ID (usually a passport or driving licence)

  • Proof of address.

6. Mortgage valuation

Lenders issue valuations to check the property is worth the amount you want to borrow for it. This is not always done in person – it may be done online, or someone may drive past the property. In some cases, lenders will charge for a valuation to be carried out.

7. Get the mortgage offer

Assuming the previous steps all go to plan, your solicitor should receive a mortgage offer letter. It may contain certain conditions that you’ll have to fulfil when the deal begins (such as paying off remaining credit card debt).

If your circumstances change between receiving the offer and moving to the new mortgage, you should tell your new lender.

8. Move to the new deal

When the time comes your solicitor will be responsible for requesting the money from your new lender, and paying off the debt owing to the old one.

They should also update Land Registry and, in some cases, the title deeds with the new details.

If you’re switching to a new deal with the same lender, some of these steps may be skipped – such as instructing a solicitor.

Where are mortgage rates now?

At the time of writing, the average two-year fixed rate is currently 5.27pc; the average five-year fix is priced slightly cheaper at 5.13pc, according to analyst Moneyfacts.

The average two-year tracker is 5.2pc, while the average SVR is 7.6pc as of April 1.

It’s possible to get much cheaper deals than the average rates, but the cheapest are often reserved for those who are remortgaging and seeking to borrow no more than 60pc of the property’s value.

One decision lots of homeowners have been struggling with is whether to commit to a fixed-rate deal, or opt for a tracker mortgage that’s linked to the Bank Rate. Should interest rates reduce further, those with tracker mortgages will see their mortgage bills get cheaper – until then, you might find yourself on a deal that’s more expensive than the cheapest fixed deals.

Our guide on the fixed rate vs tracker dilemma can help.

Will mortgages continue to rise?

Traders have bet on the Bank of England making three interest rate cuts this year which, if they happen, should mean mortgage rates will fall.

The first of these rate cuts took place on February 6, but at its most recent Monetary Policy Committee meeting on March 20 the Bank voted to hold interest rates at 4.5pc.

However, there are many factors that play into the Bank’s decision – and other market forces that affect how much lender’s charge for borrowing, such as inflation.

The consumer prices index (CPI) measure of inflation measured 2.6 in March, down slightly from 2.8pc in February. The figure is significantly above the Bank of England’s target of 2pc.

Rachel Springall, of Moneyfacts.co.uk, said: “Borrowers who are due to come off a cheap fixed rate deal will be on tenterhooks for mortgage rates to drop before they refinance, but if they have some months ahead to wait, it may be wise to consider overpaying.

“The unprecedented volatility in interest rates seen over the past five years alone may persuade borrowers to act now and secure a longer-term fixed deal for peace of mind. As has been the case for two years now, it is cheaper to lock into a five-year fixed mortgage than a two-year deal, based on average rates.”

The next Bank Rate decision is on May 8.

Where can I find the cheapest interest rates?

A mortgage broker can help find the best mortgage deals for you, but it’s also possible to do your own research.

At the time of writing, one of the cheapest two-year fixed-rate mortgages available across the UK for someone remortgaging is now 3.73pc, offered by Danske Bank, according to Moneyfacts. It is available for those with a 40pc deposit or equity, and has a £999 product fee.

Fix for five years, and one of the cheapest rates available is 3.93pc, also from Danske Bank, again for those with 40pc equity. It has a £999 product fee.

It is important to remember that the lowest interest rates do not necessarily equate to the best deal. High fees can sometimes outweigh marginal savings on similarly priced interest rates.

Lenders commonly set fees between £899 and £1,999, but some use percentages instead, such as 0.5pc of the overall loan amount.

Adrian Anderson, of broker Anderson Harris, said: “Don’t just look at the headline rate. Consider the overall cost of the deal, including any fees and whether the lender will pay for a mortgage valuation and legal conveyance, which most banks do.”

If you are locking in for longer than two years then be sure to check any repayment penalties which would apply should your circumstances change and you need to exit the deal early – these can run into the thousands of pounds.

Our mortgage increase calculator can show how much your bills will change as a result of remortgaging to a new deal.

How long should I lock in a rate for?

Thousands of today’s homeowners with fixed mortgages are still on rates agreed when the Bank Rate was far lower than it is today. This means they are likely to face a steep increase in their payments when they reach the end of their current deal and take out a new loan.

This is because while fixed rates may be falling, the cost of borrowing is still inflated when compared with mortgages which were locked in two or five years ago. Borrowers coming off those deals and searching for a new rate are very likely to face higher rates.

Brokers have been suggesting a typical borrower should fix for two years as this would minimise the amount of time spent fixed on an inflated rate. Experts are predicting a further three Bank Rate cuts by the end of 2025, with a 48pc chance of a fourth.

Nicholas Mendes of broker John Charcol said: “For borrowers evaluating their options, the choice between fixed and variable rates remains key. A fixed-rate mortgage provides stability, locking in payments for a set period, whereas a variable-rate mortgage tracks the Bank of England’s base rate, offering potential savings if rates decline further.

“For those comfortable with a slightly higher initial rate, a shorter-term fixed deal or a tracker mortgage offers flexibility, allowing a switch should rates fall further. Conversely, a longer-term fix provides certainty and protection against potential market volatility.

“Whether to wait or lock in a deal now depends on personal financial circumstances, risk tolerance, and market conditions. Seeking professional mortgage advice is crucial to making an informed decision.”

How can I get the best rate for my mortgage?

Increase your equity

Borrowers who put more equity into a property make themselves less risky customers, increasing the chances a lender will be happier to offer them lower interest rates. Banks use the term “loan-to-value ratio” (LTV) to label how much they lend a borrower against a home.

If you have a repayment mortgage, you’ll naturally increase your equity over time, but if you want to speed up the process then you can consider overpaying your mortgage.

For example, a £160,000 mortgage on a £200,000 home would be a loan-to-value of 80pc. A lower loan-to-value and bigger deposit will usually unlock lower interest rates. Mr Anderson said: “It can make quite a big difference to the amount of interest you pay over the term of the deal. So if you have cash available you may want to consider paying down part of the mortgage to access a better rate.”

Our mortgage overpayment calculator can help you weigh up whether you’re better off overpaying your mortgage, or putting your extra cash into a savings account.

Consider an offset mortgage

Households with a significant cash pile could also access lower rates by using an offset mortgage. A handful of banks allow borrowers to reduce the cost of their loan using cash held in an account with the same lender. For example, a customer borrowing a £500,000 mortgage and with £200,000 in savings would only pay interest on £300,000 of the loan, but will forfeit any interest on the cash pot.

Based on a mortgage interest rate of 4.5pc, this would reduce monthly interest from £1,873 a month to £1,124 – a saving of £749 each month.

Mr Anderson said: “Offset mortgages are proving especially popular as tax thresholds are shrinking and reducing households’ personal allowances.

“There should be no tax to pay on savings used to offset the mortgage balance and you should not be paying interest on the mortgage balance offset by the cash funds. The account should also be instant access so you still have access to liquidity if circumstances change.”

Borrowers opting for a more specialist mortgage, such as an offset deal, should consult a mortgage adviser.

What about interest-only mortgages?

Any homeowners struggling to pay their mortgage bills are able to switch to interest-only deals without a formal repayment plan. City watchdog, the Financial Conduct Authority, announced the change last year in a bid to help lenders provide mortgage forbearance at scale.

However, once the temporary interest-free period is over, homeowners must make up their repayments. To switch to a permanent interest-only deal, you’ll still need a credible repayment plan.

Broaden your horizons with award-winning British journalism. Try The Telegraph free for 1 month with unlimited access to our award-winning website, exclusive app, money-saving offers and more.
